question: In Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] AC 562, Lord Atkin formulated the neighbour principle. Which statement best describes this principle?

options.text: You must take reasonable care to avoid acts which you can reasonably foresee would be likely to injure persons in contractual privity with you
options.feedback: The neighbour principle extends beyond contractual privity — Donoghue was groundbreaking precisely because no contract existed between manufacturer and consumer.

options.text: You must take reasonable care to avoid acts or omissions which you can reasonably foresee would be likely to injure your neighbour
options.feedback: Lord Atkin’s formulation: ‘You must take reasonable care to avoid acts or omissions which you can reasonably foresee would be likely to injure your neighbour’ — Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] AC 562.

options.text: You owe a duty of care to anyone who suffers injury as a result of your actions, regardless of foreseeability
options.feedback: Foreseeability is essential. Not everyone who suffers injury can establish a duty of care.

options.text: You owe a duty of care only to those with whom you share a direct relationship of proximity in both time and space
options.feedback: Lord Atkin’s neighbour principle is broader — it covers persons ‘so closely and directly affected by my act that I ought reasonably to have them in contemplation.’

question: Three borstal boys working on an island under the supervision of three officers escape at night, board a nearby yacht, and cause damage to it while attempting to sail away. Applying Home Office v Dorset Yacht Co Ltd [1970] AC 1004, what is the legal position?

options.text: The Home Office owes no duty — borstal officers owe a duty only to the boys, not to third parties
options.feedback: The House of Lords held otherwise. The officers’ duty extended to those foreseeably affected by the boys’ escape.

options.text: The Home Office is liable — the officers owed a duty to the yacht owner as a foreseeable neighbour who might be affected by the boys’ escape
options.feedback: Correct. Home Office v Dorset Yacht Co Ltd [1970] AC 1004 held the borstal officers liable. The yacht owner was a neighbour in the Donoghue sense — sufficiently proximate and foreseeably affected.

options.text: The Home Office is liable only if the officers had actual knowledge that the specific yacht was present
options.feedback: General foreseeability of the risk (damage to yachts by escaping boys) was sufficient; specific knowledge of the particular yacht was not required.

options.text: The boys alone are liable in trespass — the officers’ omission cannot ground liability
options.feedback: While the boys were liable, the House of Lords held the officers concurrently liable for their supervisory failure.

oscoa_references: Home Office v Dorset Yacht Co Ltd [1970] AC 1004

question: The two-stage test for duty of care from Anns v Merton London Borough Council [1978] AC 728 was later superseded. What was the correct order of the two stages?

options.text: Stage 1: proximity; Stage 2: consideration of policy factors to negate the duty
options.feedback: The first stage was about a prima facie duty, not proximity alone.

options.text: Stage 1: prima facie duty on foreseeability/proximity; Stage 2: policy considerations to negate or reduce duty
options.feedback: Correct. Under Anns v Merton London Borough Council [1978] AC 728, Lord Wilberforce’s test: (1) sufficient relationship of proximity to create a prima facie duty; (2) policy considerations that might negate or limit the duty. Now superseded by Caparo.

options.text: Stage 1: consideration of policy; Stage 2: application of Donoghue neighbour principle
options.feedback: The order was reversed — proximity/neighbour principle first, then policy considerations.

options.text: Stage 1: contractual relationship; Stage 2: extension by analogy
options.feedback: The Anns test was not based on contract. It addressed tortious duties arising from proximity and policy.

oscoa_references: Anns v Merton London Borough Council [1978] AC 728

question: In Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605, the House of Lords established a three-stage test for imposing a novel duty of care. Which of the following is NOT one of the three stages?

options.text: Foreseeability of harm
options.feedback: This IS one of the three stages — the harm must be reasonably foreseeable.

options.text: A relationship of proximity between the parties
options.feedback: This IS one of the stages — there must be sufficient proximity.

options.text: A pre-existing contractual relationship
options.feedback: Correct — this is NOT one of the Caparo stages. The test operates in tort; no pre-existing contract is required.

options.text: It must be fair, just and reasonable to impose the duty
options.feedback: This IS the third stage — the court must consider whether imposing a duty is fair, just and reasonable.

oscoa_references: Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605

question: A shareholder reads a company’s audited accounts and, relying on their accuracy, buys additional shares in the company. The accounts were negligently prepared and the company later becomes insolvent. Applying Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990], does the auditor owe the shareholder a duty of care?

options.text: Yes — auditors owe a duty to all shareholders as a foreseeable class
options.feedback: Caparo specifically rejected this proposition. The audit was prepared for the shareholders as a body, not for individual investment decisions.

options.text: No — the statutory purpose of the audit was to enable shareholders to exercise governance rights, not to make individual investment decisions
options.feedback: Correct. Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605 held auditors do not owe a duty to individual shareholders for investment reliance on the audit. The audit’s statutory purpose was governance, not investment advice.

options.text: Yes — the shareholder is a member of a class to whom the auditor should have foreseen harm
options.feedback: The House of Lords held foreseeability alone was insufficient. The purpose of the statement and the relationship between the parties limited the duty.

options.text: No — shareholder claims against auditors are barred by limitation
options.feedback: Limitation was not the basis of the decision. The House of Lords held no duty of care arose on the facts.

oscoa_references: Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605

question: In Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018] UKSC 4, the Supreme Court clarified the role of Caparo. What was the correct statement of law?

options.text: The Caparo three-stage test must be applied to every duty of care analysis
options.feedback: The Supreme Court held this was the wrong approach — Caparo is not a universal test.

options.text: Where an established duty of care exists, courts should apply precedent, not re-examine through the three-stage Caparo lens
options.feedback: Correct. Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018] UKSC 4 held that Caparo is for novel situations. Established duties (e.g. positive acts causing physical injury) should be applied directly from precedent.

options.text: The police owe no duty of care in any operational circumstances
options.feedback: Robinson affirmed that the police DO owe a duty when their positive acts cause physical injury to members of the public.

options.text: Policy considerations are irrelevant after Robinson
options.feedback: Policy remains relevant in novel situations. Robinson simply rejected using Caparo to re-examine established duties.

oscoa_references: Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018] UKSC 4
Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605

question: English law’s general position on omissions is best stated as:

options.text: A person owes a general duty to take positive steps to prevent harm to others where it is reasonably practicable to do so
options.feedback: English law does NOT impose a general duty to rescue or act affirmatively — Stovin v Wise [1996] AC 923.

options.text: There is no general duty to act or to prevent harm caused by third parties, subject to recognised exceptions
options.feedback: Correct. English law does not impose a general duty to act. Exceptions include special relationships, assumption of responsibility, and creation of danger — Smith v Littlewoods Organisation Ltd [1987] AC 241.

options.text: A duty to act exists whenever the claimant is a foreseeable victim
options.feedback: Foreseeability alone is insufficient. The general rule is against liability for pure omissions.

options.text: A duty to act exists only where the defendant is a public authority
options.feedback: The rule applies to both public and private defendants. Public authorities may even have more limited duties in some contexts.

oscoa_references: Smith v Littlewoods Organisation Ltd [1987] AC 241

question: A fire brigade arrives at a burning building. The officer in charge decides to turn off the sprinkler system, making the fire significantly worse. The building owner sues for the additional damage. Applying Capital & Counties plc v Hampshire County Council [1997] QB 1004:

options.text: The fire brigade is not liable — there is no duty to respond to a fire
options.feedback: While there is no duty to attend, having attended, the fire brigade may create liability by positive acts.

options.text: The fire brigade is not liable — turning off the sprinklers was a policy decision
options.feedback: The decision was operational, not policy-based. Positive acts worsening the situation create liability.

options.text: The fire brigade is liable — having assumed responsibility by attending, the positive act of turning off the sprinklers created or worsened the danger
options.feedback: Correct. Capital & Counties plc v Hampshire County Council [1997] QB 1004 established that where a fire brigade, by positive acts, creates or increases a danger, it may owe a duty of care to those foreseeably affected.

options.text: The fire brigade is liable only if it gave an express assurance to the owner
options.feedback: Express assurance is not required. The positive act of turning off the sprinklers, which made the situation worse, was sufficient.

oscoa_references: Capital & Counties plc v Hampshire County Council [1997] QB 1004

question: In Barrett v Ministry of Defence [1995] 1 WLR 1217, a naval officer on duty found a drunk serviceman collapsed and undertook to supervise him, but failed to do so properly and the man died. Which exception to the omissions rule applied?

options.text: Creation of danger by the defendant
options.feedback: Creation of danger was not the basis on which liability was founded in Barrett.

options.text: Assumption of responsibility — the naval officer had assumed responsibility for the drunk serviceman’s welfare
options.feedback: Correct. Barrett v Ministry of Defence [1995] 1 WLR 1217 held that the officer had assumed responsibility for the serviceman’s welfare after he collapsed from drinking. The officer’s failure to supervise him properly was a breach of the assumed duty.

options.text: A contractual duty between the defendant and the claimant
options.feedback: The case was not decided on contractual grounds. The duty arose from the assumption of responsibility.

options.text: A statutory duty under the Occupiers’ Liability Act
options.feedback: The claim was not founded on occupiers’ liability but on common law negligence through assumption of responsibility.

oscoa_references: Barrett v Ministry of Defence [1995] 1 WLR 1217

question: In Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Co (1856) 11 Ex 781, Alderson B defined negligence. Which of the following accurately reflects the definition?

options.text: Negligence is the failure to exercise the skill of an expert in the defendant’s field
options.feedback: This reflects the professional negligence standard (Bolam), not the general reasonable person standard.

options.text: Negligence is the omission to do something which a reasonable man, guided by ordinary considerations, would do, or doing something which a prudent and reasonable man would not do
options.feedback: Correct. Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Co (1856) 11 Ex 781 — Alderson B’s classic formulation defines the reasonable person standard.

options.text: Negligence is any act which causes foreseeable harm to another person
options.feedback: This conflates negligence with causation. Not all acts causing foreseeable harm constitute a breach of duty.

options.text: Negligence is the failure to comply with the highest standard of care possible in the circumstances
options.feedback: The standard is reasonableness, not perfection. The law does not demand the highest possible care.

oscoa_references: Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Co (1856) 11 Ex 781

question: A learner driver causes an accident while taking a driving lesson. Her friend, who was giving her informal instruction, is injured. Applying Nettleship v Weston [1971] 2 QB 691:

options.text: The learner is held to the standard of a qualified competent driver — the standard is objective
options.feedback: Correct. Nettleship v Weston [1971] 2 QB 691 established that learner drivers are held to the standard of a reasonably competent driver. The standard is objective; inexperience is no defence.

options.text: The learner is held to the standard of a reasonable learner driver in her position
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal rejected a subjective or graduated standard. The standard is that of a competent driver.

options.text: The instructor-injured friend is volenti — she accepted the risk of injury by getting in the car
options.feedback: While volenti was considered, the Court held the standard remained objective. The friend’s knowledge of the learner’s inexperience did not lower the standard of care owed.

options.text: The learner is not liable because she lacked the experience to appreciate the risk
options.feedback: Nettleship specifically rejected this argument. Inexperience does not lower the standard of care.

question: A driver suffers a stroke while driving and causes a collision. Under Roberts v Ramsbottom [1980] 1 WLR 823, the driver’s sudden unconsciousness is a complete defence to negligence. — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: The court held that a stroke does not automatically provide a defence. The driver was still held to the objective standard of a competent driver.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. In Roberts v Ramsbottom [1980] 1 WLR 823, the defendant suffered a stroke but had continued to drive for some time. He was held to the objective standard and found in breach. The standard remains objective even for illness, unless the driver had no control whatsoever at the relevant time.

question: In Bolton v Stone [1951] AC 850, a cricket ball was hit out of a ground over a high fence and struck a passer-by. The evidence showed this had happened only about once in 30 years. The House of Lords held:

options.text: The cricket club was liable — the risk of injury was foreseeable
options.feedback: Foreseeability was established, but it was weighed against the very low likelihood of harm.

options.text: The cricket club was not in breach — despite foreseeable risk, the likelihood of injury was so small that a reasonable person would not have taken further precautions
options.feedback: Correct. Bolton v Stone [1951] AC 850 held that where the likelihood of harm is very low, a reasonable person need not take precautions. The magnitude of the risk (likelihood + seriousness) determines the standard.

options.text: The cricket club was liable — any risk of physical injury requires maximum precautions
options.feedback: This is not the law. The response demanded is proportionate to the magnitude of the risk.

options.text: The cricket club was not liable because the passer-by implicitly consented to the risk
options.feedback: The decision was based on the absence of breach, not volenti. The likelihood was too low to demand precautions.

question: A factory floor is flooded by an exceptionally heavy storm. The employer spreads sawdust and puts up warning notices but does not close the factory. A worker slips and is injured. Applying Latimer v AEC Ltd [1953] AC 643:

options.text: The employer is liable — the only reasonable response was to close the factory
options.feedback: The House of Lords held that closing the factory was disproportionate. The measures taken were reasonable.

options.text: The employer is not in breach — the steps taken were reasonable, and closing the factory would have been disproportionate to the risk
options.feedback: Correct. Latimer v AEC Ltd [1953] AC 643 held that the employer took all reasonable precautions in the circumstances. The cost and practicability of closing the entire factory outweighed the residual risk.

options.text: The employer is liable unless the sawdust was certified as non-slip
options.feedback: The court considered the reasonableness of the employer’s overall response, not regulatory certification of the specific material.

options.text: The employer is strictly liable for any injury occurring on the premises
options.feedback: Employers’ liability is fault-based, not strict. The issue was whether the employer did what was reasonable.

question: Section 1 of the Compensation Act 2006 and the Social Action, Responsibility and Heroism Act 2015 were both enacted following concerns about ‘compensation culture.’ What do these statutes require courts to consider?

options.text: Whether the defendant had comprehensive liability insurance
options.feedback: Insurance is not a factor under either statute. The focus is on social value, not insurance status.

options.text: Whether a finding of breach would prevent socially desirable activities, and whether the defendant was acting for society’s benefit, responsibly, or heroically
options.feedback: Correct. Compensation Act 2006 s.1 requires courts to consider whether precautions would prevent desirable activities. SARAH Act 2015 requires consideration of whether the defendant acted for society’s benefit, demonstrated responsible conduct, or acted heroically.

options.text: Whether the claimant consented to the risk
options.feedback: Consent is the domain of volenti, not these statutes. The statutes address the breach standard itself.

options.text: Whether the defendant is a public or private body
options.feedback: Neither statute distinguishes based on the defendant’s public/private status. They apply generally.

oscoa_references: Social Action, Responsibility and Heroism Act 2015

question: Under Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957] 1 WLR 582, a doctor is not negligent if:

options.text: The doctor acted to the best of their personal ability
options.feedback: The test is not subjective. It focuses on whether a responsible body of professional opinion would have acted similarly.

options.text: The doctor acted in accordance with a practice accepted as proper by a responsible body of medical opinion
options.feedback: Correct. The Bolam test: a doctor is not negligent if acting in accordance with a practice accepted as proper by a responsible body of medical men skilled in that particular art — Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957] 1 WLR 582.

options.text: The doctor obtained the patient’s fully informed consent to the treatment
options.feedback: Consent goes to the tort of battery, not the standard of care in negligence. Bolam addresses the standard of professional skill.

options.text: The doctor followed guidelines issued by the General Medical Council
options.feedback: GMC guidelines are evidence of proper practice but do not define the legal standard. The Bolam test considers responsible professional opinion more broadly.

oscoa_references: Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957] 1 WLR 582

question: In Bolitho v City and Hackney Health Authority [1997] UKHL 41, the House of Lords added an important qualification to the Bolam test. Which of the following correctly expresses that qualification?

options.text: The professional body whose opinion is relied upon must be the majority of practitioners in the field
options.feedback: The Bolam test refers to a ‘responsible’ body, not necessarily a majority. Bolitho did not change this.

options.text: The professional opinion relied upon must be capable of withstanding logical analysis — it is not enough for experts to simply say they would have done the same
options.feedback: Correct. Bolitho v City and Hackney Health Authority [1997] UKHL 41 held that the court must be satisfied that the body of opinion relied upon has a logical basis. Expert evidence is not conclusive merely because it is tendered.

options.text: The Bolam test only applies to diagnosis, not to treatment
options.feedback: The Bolam test applies to all aspects of medical practice. Bolitho did not restrict its scope.

options.text: Courts must defer to expert opinion in all cases involving complex medical judgments
options.feedback: Bolitho empowers courts to scrutinise expert opinion. The court is the ultimate arbiter, not a passive recipient of expert views.

oscoa_references: Bolitho v City and Hackney Health Authority [1997] UKHL 41
Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957] 1 WLR 582

question: A pregnant diabetic woman is not warned of the 9–10% risk of shoulder dystocia during vaginal delivery, which is a serious complication. The risk materialises and the baby is injured. Applying Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] UKSC 11:

options.text: The doctor is not negligent — disclosure of risk is governed by Bolam; a responsible body of doctors might not have warned of the risk
options.feedback: Montgomery rejected Bolam for consent cases. The standard is patient-centred, not doctor-centred.

options.text: The doctor is negligent — the test is whether a reasonable person in the patient’s position would be likely to attach significance to the risk
options.feedback: Correct. Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] UKSC 11 established that the standard for risk disclosure is patient-centred. A material risk is one which a reasonable person in the patient’s position would likely attach significance to, or which the doctor should reasonably be aware the particular patient would attach significance to.

options.text: The doctor is not negligent if the risk was below 10%
options.feedback: No percentage threshold applies. The test is the significance of the risk to the patient, not statistical frequency.

options.text: The doctor is only negligent if the patient specifically asked about the risk
options.feedback: Montgomery does not require the patient to ask. The doctor has a proactive duty to disclose material risks.

oscoa_references: Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] UKSC 11

question: In South Australia Asset Management Corp v York Montague Ltd [1997] AC 191 (SAAMCO), the House of Lords distinguished between two types of professional duty. Which correctly states the distinction and consequence?

options.text: Advisory duty: professional liable for all foreseeable losses; Information duty: professional liable only for losses caused by the information being wrong
options.feedback: Correct. SAAMCO v York Montague Ltd [1997] AC 191 held that a professional providing information (not advice) is liable only for consequences of the information being wrong — not all losses flowing from the transaction. A valuer providing an over-valuation provides information, not full advice.

options.text: Advisory duty: professional liable only if the advice is in writing; Information duty: professional liable for all oral statements
options.feedback: The form (written vs oral) is not the distinction. The key question is whether the professional assumed responsibility for the whole transaction or only for the accuracy of specific information.

options.text: There is no practical distinction — professionals are liable for all losses flowing from their negligence
options.feedback: The SAAMCO principle significantly limits professional liability. Professionals are not liable for all losses that result from the claimant entering the transaction.

options.text: Advisory duty: professional is a fiduciary; Information duty: professional is not a fiduciary
options.feedback: SAAMCO was not about fiduciary status. It concerned the scope of the duty of care in negligence.

oscoa_references: South Australia Asset Management Corp v York Montague Ltd [1997] AC 191

question: The ‘but-for’ test for factual causation is demonstrated by Barnett v Chelsea and Kensington Hospital Management Committee [1969] 1 QB 428. What was the outcome?

options.text: The hospital was liable because it negligently failed to examine the deceased
options.feedback: Despite the hospital’s breach (failing to examine), causation was not established.

options.text: The hospital was not liable — even if the doctor had examined the deceased, he would have died from arsenic poisoning before treatment could save him
options.feedback: Correct. Barnett v Chelsea and Kensington Hospital Management Committee [1969] 1 QB 428 — the doctor’s omission did not cause the death. Applying the but-for test, the man would have died even if the doctor had seen him. Causation failed.

options.text: The hospital was liable — a doctor’s failure to examine a patient is always causative of subsequent harm
options.feedback: This is not correct. Even where there is a breach of duty, the breach must cause the damage. In Barnett, the arsenic was already fatal.

options.text: The hospital was not liable because the deceased voluntarily assumed the risk of poisoning
options.feedback: Volenti was not the issue. The outcome turned on the but-for test of factual causation.

oscoa_references: Barnett v Chelsea and Kensington Hospital Management Committee [1969] 1 QB 428

question: In Fairchild v Glenhaven Funeral Services Ltd [2002] UKHL 22, the House of Lords created an exception to the standard but-for test for causation in mesothelioma cases. What was the exception?

options.text: Where multiple defendants have exposed a claimant to asbestos, liability is apportioned based on market share
options.feedback: The market share approach of US law was not adopted. The House of Lords took a different path.

options.text: The claimant need only prove that the defendant’s tortious conduct materially increased the risk of contracting the disease — each defendant is jointly and severally liable
options.feedback: Correct. Fairchild v Glenhaven Funeral Services Ltd [2002] UKHL 22 held that in mesothelioma cases, where the but-for test is unworkable due to scientific uncertainty, a claimant can establish causation by proving the defendant’s breach materially increased the risk of contracting the disease.

options.text: The claimant must prove on a balance of probabilities that a specific defendant’s asbestos was the cause
options.feedback: Fairchild relaxed this requirement because it is scientifically impossible to prove which single asbestos fibre caused mesothelioma.

options.text: Employers are strictly liable for all occupational diseases, regardless of causation
options.feedback: Strict liability does not apply. The Fairchild exception relaxes causation, not fault.

oscoa_references: Fairchild v Glenhaven Funeral Services Ltd [2002] UKHL 22

question: A worker contracts pneumoconiosis from inhaling silica dust at work. The dust came from two sources: (1) a pneumatic hammer with a lawful dust level, and (2) grinding wheels with an unlawfully high dust level. The disease is indivisible — each exposure contributed cumulatively. Applying Bonnington Castings Ltd v Wardlaw [1956] AC 613:

options.text: The claimant must prove that the unlawful dust alone would have caused the disease on a balance of probabilities
options.feedback: This standard would be impossible to meet in indivisible disease cases. Bonnington adopted a lower threshold.

options.text: The employer is liable — the claimant need only prove that the unlawful dust materially contributed to the disease
options.feedback: Correct. Bonnington Castings Ltd v Wardlaw [1956] AC 613 held that for indivisible diseases, a claimant need only show that the tortious exposure made a material contribution to the harm. This is less demanding than the but-for test.

options.text: The employer is not liable because the lawful dust alone could have caused the disease
options.feedback: The House of Lords rejected this argument. The fact that some dust was lawful does not exonerate the employer where tortious dust contributed materially.

options.text: The employer is liable only for a percentage of damages corresponding to the proportion of unlawful dust
options.feedback: Apportionment was not required. The employer was fully liable because the unlawful dust materially contributed to the indivisible disease.

oscoa_references: Bonnington Castings Ltd v Wardlaw [1956] AC 613

question: In Knightley v Johns [1982] 1 WLR 349, a traffic accident in a tunnel was followed by a police officer negligently ordering a motorcyclist to ride the wrong way back through the tunnel, where he was struck and killed. The negligent order was held to be:

options.text: A foreseeable consequence for which the original driver remained liable
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal held the police officer’s negligent decision broke the chain.

options.text: A novus actus interveniens that broke the chain of causation from the original collision
options.feedback: Correct. Knightley v Johns [1982] 1 WLR 349 held that the senior police officer’s negligent instruction to ride against traffic was a new intervening act that broke the chain of causation from the original negligent driver.

options.text: Irrelevant — the original driver was liable for all consequences flowing from the initial accident
options.feedback: Not all consequences flowing from an accident are attributable to the original tortfeasor if a later event breaks the chain.

options.text: An act of the claimant that broke the chain
options.feedback: The motorcyclist was not the one who broke the chain. The negligent order by the senior officer was the novus actus.

question: After a workplace accident, a worker has an injured leg that sometimes gives way. While descending a staircase without a handrail and holding his child, his leg gives way. He jumps to avoid injuring the child, sustaining further injury. Applying McKew v Holland & Hannen & Cubitts (Scotland) Ltd [1969] 3 All ER 1621:

options.text: The employer is liable for the further injury — the original injury was a cause of the later fall
options.feedback: The House of Lords held that the claimant’s unreasonable conduct broke the chain of causation.

options.text: The employer is not liable — the claimant’s unreasonable decision to descend steep stairs without assistance broke the chain of causation
options.feedback: Correct. McKew v Holland & Hannen & Cubitts (Scotland) Ltd [1969] 3 All ER 1621 — the claimant’s own unreasonable conduct in descending stairs unassisted, knowing his leg might give way, constituted a novus actus interveniens.

options.text: The employer is liable under the thin skull rule for the full extent of the injuries
options.feedback: The thin skull rule applies to pre-existing vulnerabilities, not to unreasonable conduct by the claimant after the accident.

options.text: The employer is liable unless the staircase was compliant with building regulations
options.feedback: The condition of the staircase was not the determinative issue. The claimant’s own unreasonable behaviour was.

oscoa_references: McKew v Holland & Hannen & Cubitts (Scotland) Ltd [1969] 3 All ER 1621

question: In Webb v Barclays Bank plc [2001] EWCA Civ 1141, the claimant’s original injury was exacerbated by a second doctor’s negligent treatment. The Court of Appeal held:

options.text: The second doctor’s negligence was a novus actus — the original tortfeasor was not liable for the additional harm
options.feedback: The Court held the opposite — medical negligence in treatment of the original injury is generally not a novus actus.

options.text: Subsequent medical negligence is always a novus actus if it is ‘gross’
options.feedback: The law does not distinguish based on the degree of medical negligence. The key is whether the negligence is a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the original injury.

options.text: The original tortfeasor remained liable — ordinary medical negligence in treating the original injury does not break the chain of causation
options.feedback: Correct. Webb v Barclays Bank plc [2001] EWCA Civ 1141 held that negligent medical treatment of the original injury is generally not a novus actus. The original tortfeasor remains liable for the aggregate harm.

options.text: Liability was apportioned between the original tortfeasor and the second doctor
options.feedback: The original tortfeasor was held fully liable. The claimant could also sue the second doctor, but the original defendant could not shift liability.

oscoa_references: Webb v Barclays Bank plc [2001] EWCA Civ 1141

question: In Overseas Tankship (UK) Ltd v Morts Dock and Engineering Co Ltd (The Wagon Mound) (No 1) [1961] AC 388, the Privy Council established the test for remoteness. What is the test?

options.text: Damage of the precise type and extent must have been reasonably foreseeable
options.feedback: The test requires foreseeability of the type of damage, not the precise extent or manner — Hughes v Lord Advocate [1963].

options.text: Damage of the same type or kind must have been reasonably foreseeable — the precise extent or manner need not be
options.feedback: Correct. The Wagon Mound (No 1) [1961] AC 388 — the test for remoteness is reasonable foreseeability of the type of damage. Furnace oil on water was foreseeable to cause fouling but not fire. The dock owner could recover for fouling damage but not fire damage.

options.text: Any damage flowing directly from the breach is recoverable, regardless of foreseeability
options.feedback: This was the old direct consequences test from Re Polemis, which Wagon Mound overruled.

options.text: Damage must have been the inevitable consequence of the breach
options.feedback: The test is reasonable foreseeability, not inevitability. A much lower threshold.

oscoa_references: Overseas Tankship (UK) Ltd v Morts Dock and Engineering Co Ltd (The Wagon Mound) (No 1) [1961] AC 388

question: In Hughes v Lord Advocate [1963] AC 837, workmen left an open manhole covered by a tent with paraffin lamps nearby. A child entered the tent, and one of the lamps exploded, causing severe burns. The House of Lords held the explosion was:

options.text: Too remote — an explosion was not the foreseeable type of harm from leaving paraffin lamps
options.feedback: The House of Lords held that the type of harm (burning) was foreseeable, even if the manner (explosion) was not specifically foreseen.

options.text: Not too remote — the type of harm (burning) was foreseeable; the precise manner (explosion) need not have been foreseeable
options.feedback: Correct. Hughes v Lord Advocate [1963] AC 837 — the workmen should have foreseen that the child might be burned by the lamps. The explosion was simply a variation on the foreseeable type of harm (burn injury).

options.text: Not too remote — the workmen had actual knowledge of the risk of explosion
options.feedback: The workmen did not have actual knowledge of explosion risk. The decision was based on foreseeability of the general type of harm.

options.text: Too remote — the child was a trespasser and no duty was owed
options.feedback: The duty to the child arose under the principle that allurement to children may give rise to a duty, not from the Wagon Mound test.

oscoa_references: Overseas Tankship (UK) Ltd v Morts Dock and Engineering Co Ltd (The Wagon Mound) (No 1) [1961] AC 388

question: A farm worker contracts Weil’s disease from contact with rat urine on a farm with a known rat infestation. The employer had failed to control the infestation. The worker sues. Applying Tremain v Pike [1969] 1 WLR 1556, what is the likely result on remoteness?

options.text: The damage is not too remote — any harm from rat infestation is foreseeable
options.feedback: The court drew a distinction between types of harm. Physical injury from a rat bite is one type; disease from rat urine is quite another.

options.text: The disease is too remote — the foreseeable type of harm from rat infestation was injury (e.g. rat bites), not Weil’s disease contracted from rat urine
options.feedback: Correct. Tremain v Pike [1969] 1 WLR 1556 held that Weil’s disease contracted from rat urine was a different type of harm from the foreseeable harm of injury from rat infestation. The disease was too remote.

options.text: The disease is not too remote because the infestation was the factual cause of the disease
options.feedback: Factual causation (but-for) is a separate inquiry from remoteness. The disease was factually caused but legally too remote.

options.text: The damage is not too remote if Weil’s disease is a known occupational risk for farm workers
options.feedback: The court focused on the foreseeability of the type of harm from the perspective of the employer dealing with a rat infestation, not general occupational knowledge.

question: In Smith v Leech Brain & Co Ltd [1962] 2 QB 405, a workman was splashed with molten metal on his lip. The burn triggered a pre-malignant cancerous condition from which he died. The employer was held liable for his death. This illustrates:

options.text: The egg-shell skull (thin skull) rule — a tortfeasor must take their victim as they find them
options.feedback: Correct. Smith v Leech Brain & Co Ltd [1962] 2 QB 405 — the thin skull rule. The employer was liable for the full extent of the harm, including the cancer triggered by the burn, even though the cancer was unforeseeable.

options.text: The Bolam test for medical causation
options.feedback: Bolam concerns the standard of care for professionals, not the scope of liability for harm.

options.text: The principle that employers are insurers against all workplace injuries
options.feedback: Employers are not strict insurers. Liability requires fault, but once established, the thin skull rule governs the extent.

options.text: The principle that pre-existing conditions break the chain of causation
options.feedback: The thin skull rule operates in the opposite direction — pre-existing conditions do NOT break the chain. The defendant is liable for the full extent.

oscoa_references: Smith v Leech Brain & Co Ltd [1962] 2 QB 405

question: A man suffers minor physical injuries in a car accident caused by another driver’s negligence. He had a history of ME (chronic fatigue syndrome), and the accident triggers a severe relapse, leaving him permanently disabled. Applying Page v Smith [1996] AC 155:

options.text: The defendant is only liable for the minor physical injuries — the psychiatric deterioration was unforeseeable
options.feedback: Page v Smith extended the thin skull rule to psychiatric harm in primary victims. The defendant is liable for the full extent.

options.text: The defendant is liable for the full psychiatric injury — the thin skull rule applies to psychiatric as well as physical vulnerability in primary victims
options.feedback: Correct. Page v Smith [1996] AC 155 — for primary victims (those within the zone of physical danger), the thin skull rule extends to psychiatric vulnerability. The defendant takes the claimant as found, including psychiatric frailty.

options.text: The defendant is not liable — the ME was not caused by the accident
options.feedback: The ME was pre-existing but the accident triggered the relapse. The thin skull rule catches aggravation of pre-existing conditions.

options.text: The defendant is liable only if the psychiatric outcome was a reasonably foreseeable type of harm
options.feedback: For primary victims, if physical injury is foreseeable, psychiatric harm need not be. The thin skull rule obviates the need to show foreseeability of the psychiatric harm.

question: In Lagden v O’Connor [2003] UKHL 64, the claimant could not afford to hire a replacement car after the defendant negligently damaged his vehicle, so he incurred credit hire charges at a higher rate. The House of Lords held:

options.text: The defendant need only pay the standard hire rate — impecuniosity is irrelevant to damages
options.feedback: The majority held otherwise. A defendant must take the claimant’s impecuniosity into account as a form of thin skull.

options.text: The defendant must take the claimant as found, including his impecuniosity — the credit hire charges were recoverable
options.feedback: Correct. Lagden v O’Connor [2003] UKHL 64 — the House of Lords extended the thin skull principle to impecuniosity. The defendant had to take the claimant’s financial vulnerability as found and was liable for the reasonable costs of mitigating within the claimant’s means.

options.text: The credit hire charges were too remote — a defendant cannot foresee the claimant’s personal finances
options.feedback: The thin skull rule in this context operates irrespective of foreseeability. Impecuniosity is not too remote.

options.text: The defendant is only liable if the claimant disclosed his financial position before the accident
options.feedback: No such disclosure requirement exists. The defendant takes the claimant as found, regardless of prior knowledge.

question: Who is a primary victim for the purposes of psychiatric injury in negligence?

options.text: Any person who witnesses a traumatic event
options.feedback: Witnessing alone typically makes a person a secondary victim (subject to Alcock controls), not a primary victim.

options.text: A person within the zone of physical danger who suffers psychiatric injury as a result of the defendant’s negligence
options.feedback: Correct. Page v Smith [1996] AC 155 — primary victims are those within the range of foreseeable physical injury. Where physical injury is foreseeable, recovery for psychiatric harm does not require foreseeability of psychiatric harm.

options.text: Only a person who sustains both physical and psychiatric injury
options.feedback: Page v Smith allowed recovery for pure psychiatric harm without physical injury, where physical injury was foreseeable.

options.text: Any person with a close tie of love and affection to someone physically injured
options.feedback: This describes secondary victims (under Alcock), not primary victims.

question: A pub patron looks up to see a horse-drawn cart crashing through the wall towards her. She dives clear just in time, avoiding physical injury, but suffers severe psychiatric shock and gives premature birth. Applying Dulieu v White & Sons [1901] 2 KB 669:

options.text: She cannot recover — there was no physical impact
options.feedback: Dulieu v White established that psychiatric harm from reasonable fear of immediate physical injury is recoverable without physical impact.

options.text: She can recover as a primary victim — shock from reasonable fear of immediate physical injury is recoverable
options.feedback: Correct. Dulieu v White & Sons [1901] 2 KB 669 held that psychiatric injury arising from reasonable fear for one’s own safety (being within the zone of physical danger) is recoverable in negligence.

options.text: She cannot recover — psychiatric harm requires proof of physical injury
options.feedback: The case itself predates this restriction; and Page v Smith confirms primary victims need not prove physical injury if physical injury was foreseeable.

options.text: She can recover only if the horse-drawn cart was a foreseeable danger
options.feedback: Foreseeability of physical injury was established. The question was whether psychiatric harm unaccompanied by physical injury was recoverable.

question: In White v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire Police [1999] 2 AC 455, the House of Lords held that all rescuers are automatically primary victims. — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: The House of Lords rejected the proposition that rescuers are automatically primary victims. Rescuers must still show they were objectively exposed to physical danger.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. White v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire Police [1999] 2 AC 455 — rescuers are not a special category. They must satisfy the same test as other primary victims: they must have been objectively within the zone of physical danger.

oscoa_references: White v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire Police [1999] 2 AC 455

question: In Alcock v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire Police [1992] 1 AC 310 (the Hillsborough case), the House of Lords identified control mechanisms for secondary victims. Which of the following are required? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: A close tie of love and affection with the primary victim
options.feedback: Correct. There must be a close tie of love and affection — presumed for parent/child and spouse, otherwise must be proved.

options.text: Proximity in time and space — presence at the scene or its immediate aftermath
options.feedback: Correct. The secondary victim must perceive the event or its immediate aftermath with their own unaided senses.

options.text: Psychiatric injury must be caused by perceiving the event through own unaided senses (not television or third-party account)
options.feedback: Correct. The shock must come through sight or hearing of the event or its immediate aftermath directly.

options.text: Physical injury to the secondary victim
options.feedback: A secondary victim need not suffer physical injury. The claim is for psychiatric harm, though it must amount to a recognised psychiatric illness.

options.text: Proof that the defendant intended to cause psychiatric harm
options.feedback: This is not required. The claim is in negligence, not intentional infliction of harm. Foreseeability of psychiatric injury is the standard.

oscoa_references: Alcock v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire Police [1992] 1 AC 310

question: A mother is informed her family has been in a car accident. She rushes to the hospital, arriving two hours after the crash. She sees her family in their injured state before they have been treated and cleaned up. She suffers psychiatric injury. Applying McLoughlin v O’Brian [1983] 1 AC 410:

options.text: She cannot recover — she was not at the scene of the accident
options.feedback: The House of Lords extended ‘immediate aftermath’ to include the hospital scene.

options.text: She can recover — seeing her family at the hospital two hours after the crash falls within the ‘immediate aftermath’
options.feedback: Correct. McLoughlin v O’Brian [1983] 1 AC 410 — the hospital visit two hours after the accident was held to be within the ‘immediate aftermath.’ The family were still in the condition caused by the accident.

options.text: She cannot recover — only the accident scene itself qualifies
options.feedback: McLoughlin itself expanded the concept beyond the accident scene to include the immediate aftermath.

options.text: She can recover under the primary victim rules, as a mother
options.feedback: She was a secondary victim (not within the zone of physical danger herself). The Court applied the Alcock-type controls, though pre-dating Alcock.

question: In Taylor v A Novo (UK) Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 194, a daughter witnessed her mother’s death at hospital following a workplace accident. The mother died three weeks after the accident. The Court of Appeal held:

options.text: The daughter could recover — hospital attendance is within the immediate aftermath
options.feedback: The Court distinguished McLoughlin and denied recovery.

options.text: The daughter could not recover — the death occurred three weeks after the accident, not in the immediate aftermath; the relevant ‘event’ was the accident, not the death
options.feedback: Correct. Taylor v A Novo (UK) Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 194 — the relevant event was the workplace accident, not the mother’s later death. Seeing her mother die three weeks later was outside the immediate aftermath of the accident.

options.text: The daughter could recover if the employer admitted liability for the mother’s death
options.feedback: Liability for the mother’s death was not the issue. The question was whether the daughter met the Alcock control mechanisms.

options.text: The daughter could not recover because she was not within the zone of physical danger
options.feedback: The daughter was a secondary victim. The issue on appeal was whether the hospital attendance constituted the immediate aftermath.

oscoa_references: Taylor v A Novo (UK) Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 194

question: In Spartan Steel & Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27, a contractor negligently cut a power cable. The factory suffered three types of loss. Which of the following was NOT recoverable?

options.text: Damage to the metal in the furnace at the time of the power cut (physical damage to property)
options.feedback: This was recoverable — it was consequential economic loss flowing from physical damage to property.

options.text: Loss of profit on the metal that was in the furnace and was damaged (consequential economic loss)
options.feedback: This was recoverable — profit loss consequent on physical damage to property.

options.text: Loss of profit on metal that would have been processed during the time the factory was without power (pure economic loss)
options.feedback: Correct. This was pure economic loss — not flowing from physical damage to the claimant’s property, but from the inability to use the factory. It was irrecoverable under the exclusionary rule in Spartan Steel.

options.text: All three types of loss were recoverable
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal held that only losses (a) and (b) were recoverable. The pure economic loss (c) was not.

oscoa_references: Spartan Steel & Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27

question: Consequential economic loss flowing from physical damage to the claimant’s own property is recoverable in negligence, even though pure economic loss is generally not. — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: Correct. Spartan Steel & Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27 — consequential economic loss (e.g. lost profits due to damaged property) is recoverable. Pure economic loss (loss not flowing from physical damage) is not, unless an exception applies.

options.text: False
options.feedback: The statement is correct. Consequential economic loss is distinguished from pure economic loss. The former is recoverable; the latter is generally not.

oscoa_references: Spartan Steel & Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27

question: A supplier negligently delivers defective machine parts. The parts themselves do not cause physical damage to any other property, but they are worthless and the buyer must purchase replacements at a higher price. The buyer sues in tort for the cost of replacement. Applying the rule against pure economic loss:

options.text: The buyer can recover in tort — the defective parts are physical property that has been damaged
options.feedback: Defective products that cause no damage to other property give rise to pure economic loss — the loss is in the product itself being worth less than paid for.

options.text: The buyer cannot recover in tort — this is pure economic loss; the buyer’s remedy lies in contract, not tort
options.feedback: Correct. The loss is purely economic — the defective product is worth less than expected. The buyer’s remedy is in contract for breach of the sale agreement. Tort does not fill gaps in contractual protection for pure economic loss.

options.text: The buyer can recover — defective products are inherently dangerous and attract a duty of care
options.feedback: Even under Donoghue v Stevenson, the duty arises for dangerous defects causing personal injury or property damage. Mere defectiveness causing economic loss is contractual.

options.text: The buyer cannot recover because there was no contractual relationship with the supplier
options.feedback: If there is no contract at all, the claim would fail for lack of contractual relationship AND the tort exclusionary rule.

oscoa_references: Spartan Steel & Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27

question: In Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465, Heller & Partners was not liable because of an effective disclaimer. However, the House of Lords established that a duty of care for negligent misstatement could arise where:

options.text: The statement was made in the course of business
options.feedback: The duty requires more than a business context. A special relationship is needed.

options.text: There was a special relationship of trust and confidence between the parties — a voluntary assumption of responsibility by the defendant, and reasonable reliance by the claimant
options.feedback: Correct. Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465 — liability for negligent misstatement arises where there is a special relationship equivalent to contract, involving a voluntary assumption of responsibility and reasonable reliance by the claimant.

options.text: The statement was made in writing and signed by the defendant
options.feedback: A written signed statement is not a prerequisite. Oral statements can attract liability under the Hedley Byrne principle.

options.text: The defendant was a professional person holding a recognised qualification
options.feedback: Professional qualification is not essential, though it is a relevant factor in determining whether a special relationship exists.

oscoa_references: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465

question: Managing agents at Lloyd’s negligently underwrite insurance on behalf of Lloyd’s Names, causing massive losses. Some Names have a direct contractual relationship with the agents; others do not (they are indirect Names who contracted through members’ agents). Applying Henderson v Merrett Syndicates Ltd [1995] 2 AC 145:

options.text: Neither group can sue in tort — the relationship is exclusively governed by contract
options.feedback: The House of Lords held there was concurrent liability in contract and tort for direct Names, and tort liability for indirect Names.

options.text: Both groups can sue in tort — the managing agents assumed responsibility for all Names, regardless of direct contractual privity
options.feedback: Correct. Henderson v Merrett Syndicates Ltd [1995] 2 AC 145 — the managing agents assumed responsibility to both direct and indirect Names. Tort liability existed alongside contract for direct Names and independently for indirect Names.

options.text: Only the direct Names can sue in tort — indirect Names have no relationship with the agents
options.feedback: The House of Lords held the agents had assumed responsibility to all Names, extending Hedley Byrne to fill the contractual gap.

options.text: The agents are not liable to anyone in tort — the loss is pure economic loss
options.feedback: Pure economic loss was recoverable because the case fell within the Hedley Byrne exception — voluntary assumption of responsibility.

oscoa_references: Henderson v Merrett Syndicates Ltd [1995] 2 AC 145
Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465

question: In Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605, the auditors’ report on a company’s accounts was held not to create a duty to individual shareholders for investment decisions. What principle did the House of Lords apply?

options.text: The disclaimer principle — auditors always exclude liability through engagement letters
options.feedback: The decision was not based on disclaimers. The court held that the purpose of the audit defined the scope of the duty.

options.text: The purpose of the statement defines the scope of the duty — the audit was for governance purposes, not individual investment decisions
options.feedback: Correct. Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605 — the purpose for which the statement was made (statutory audit for governance) limited the scope of the duty. It was not reasonably foreseeable that individual shareholders would rely on it for investment.

options.text: The privity of contract rule — only those in contractual relationships can recover
options.feedback: Hedley Byrne had already established that liability could arise outside contract. The issue was the scope of the duty, not privity.

options.text: The limitation rule — the shareholders’ claims were time-barred
options.feedback: Limitation was not the basis of the decision. The House of Lords decided the case on duty of care grounds.

oscoa_references: Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605
Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465

question: In Stovin v Wise [1996] AC 923, a local authority knew a road junction was dangerous but failed to exercise its statutory power to improve it. A collision occurred. The House of Lords held:

options.text: The local authority was liable — knowledge of danger created a duty to act
options.feedback: The House of Lords held that mere knowledge of a danger, without more, does not create a common law duty to act.

options.text: The local authority was not liable — there is no general duty to act, and failure to exercise a statutory power is an omission, not a positive act
options.feedback: Correct. Stovin v Wise [1996] AC 923 — the failure to exercise a statutory power to improve a junction was an omission, not a positive act. There was no duty to act, and the statutory power did not give rise to a common law duty of care.

options.text: The local authority was liable — public authorities must exercise all their statutory powers
options.feedback: Statutory powers are typically discretionary. A failure to exercise a power is treated as an omission for which no liability generally arises.

options.text: The local authority and the negligent driver were jointly liable
options.feedback: The local authority was not liable. Only the negligent driver bore liability.

question: A property developer owns a disused cinema. Vandals repeatedly break in, and this is known to the developer. The developer does not board up or demolish the building. Vandals eventually start a fire that spreads to a neighbouring property. The neighbour sues. Applying Smith v Littlewoods Organisation Ltd [1987] AC 241:

options.text: The developer is liable — a foreseeable danger should have been prevented
options.feedback: The House of Lords held there was no duty to prevent the criminal acts of third parties.

options.text: The developer is not liable — there is no general duty to prevent third-party criminal acts, and the developer had not created the danger
options.feedback: Correct. Smith v Littlewoods Organisation Ltd [1987] AC 241 — the general rule is that a person owes no duty to prevent harm caused by the criminal acts of third parties. The developer did not create the danger; the vandals did.

options.text: The developer is liable under the Occupiers’ Liability Act 1984
options.feedback: The 1984 Act did not apply to the neighbour’s claim because the neighbour was not a trespasser. The claim was in common law negligence.

options.text: The developer is liable because arson is always a foreseeable risk in disused buildings
options.feedback: Foreseeability alone is not sufficient. The House of Lords emphasised the distinction between acts and omissions.

oscoa_references: Smith v Littlewoods Organisation Ltd [1987] AC 241

question: In Poole Borough Council v GN [2019] UKSC 25, the Supreme Court addressed whether a local authority owed a common law duty of care to children in its area. What was the correct analysis?

options.text: The council owed a duty — children are a vulnerable group requiring special protection
options.feedback: The Supreme Court rejected this approach. Vulnerability alone does not create a duty.

options.text: The issue was approached as an omissions problem — the council’s failure to remove children from harm was an omission for which no duty arose without an assumption of responsibility
options.feedback: Correct. Poole Borough Council v GN [2019] UKSC 25 — the Supreme Court held the correct analysis was that the council’s failure to act was an omission. No duty arose unless the council had assumed responsibility or created the danger.

options.text: The council owed a duty because it had statutory powers to protect children
options.feedback: Statutory powers do not, without more, create a common law duty of care — Stovin v Wise [1996].

options.text: The council owed a duty under the Human Rights Act 1998
options.feedback: The Supreme Court was considering the common law duty of care, not the HRA. The two are separate claims.

oscoa_references: Poole Borough Council v GN [2019] UKSC 25

question: In Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018] UKSC 4, police officers attempted to arrest a suspect, causing an elderly passer-by to be knocked over and injured. The Supreme Court held:

options.text: The police owed no duty — public authorities are immune from negligence claims in operational matters
options.feedback: There is no blanket immunity for the police. Robinson confirmed that the police owe a duty for positive acts causing physical injury.

options.text: The police owed a duty of care — the injury was caused by a positive act (the arrest), not an omission, and this fell within an established duty category
options.feedback: Correct. Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018] UKSC 4 — police owe a duty of care for positive acts causing physical injury to members of the public. This was an established category not requiring Caparo analysis.

options.text: The police owed a duty — the Caparo three-stage test was satisfied
options.feedback: The Supreme Court held the Caparo test was unnecessary because the duty fell within an established category.

options.text: The police owed no duty — the injury was merely an unfortunate accident in the course of lawful police work
options.feedback: The Supreme Court found the police liable. The officers’ positive actions during the arrest directly caused the injury.

oscoa_references: Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018] UKSC 4

question: A woman calls 999 reporting that her ex-partner has threatened to kill her. The call handler classifies it as a lower-priority call. The police arrive 15 minutes later; by then the woman has been murdered. Applying Michael v Chief Constable of South Wales Police [2015] UKSC 2:

options.text: The police are liable — they assumed responsibility by taking the 999 call
options.feedback: The Supreme Court held that taking a 999 call does not, without more, give rise to an assumption of responsibility.

options.text: The police are not liable — the failure to respond with sufficient speed was an omission, and policy considerations (including defensive policing) militated against imposing a duty
options.feedback: Correct. Michael v Chief Constable of South Wales Police [2015] UKSC 2 — the police’s failure to arrive sooner was an omission. Policy reasons, including the risk of defensive policing and diversion of resources, weighed against imposing a duty of care.

options.text: The police are liable — the Article 2 right to life imposes a positive operational duty on the police
options.feedback: Article 2 arguments were considered, but the claim failed on common law negligence grounds. The Article 2 operational duty is distinct.

options.text: The police are not liable because the ex-partner’s criminal act was a novus actus interveniens
options.feedback: The criminal act was not a novus actus — it was the very harm the call was about. The issue was whether the police owed a duty of care, not causation.

oscoa_references: Michael v Chief Constable of South Wales Police [2015] UKSC 2

question: In X (Minors) v Bedfordshire County Council [1995] 2 AC 633, the House of Lords considered claims against social services for negligent investigation and handling of child abuse allegations. What was the outcome on the common law negligence claim?

options.text: The claims succeeded — social services owe a common law duty to children referred to them
options.feedback: The House of Lords struck out the claims. No common law duty of care was owed.

options.text: The claims were struck out — no common law duty of care was owed for investigation and removal decisions by social services; alternative remedy lay under the Human Rights Act
options.feedback: Correct. X (Minors) v Bedfordshire County Council [1995] 2 AC 633 — social services did not owe a common law duty of care for their child protection functions. The House of Lords noted that redress might lie under human rights legislation (now HRA 1998).

options.text: The claims succeeded under Caparo Industries v Dickman
options.feedback: The House of Lords held that imposing a duty would not be fair, just and reasonable, given the statutory framework and the multi-disciplinary nature of child protection work.

options.text: The claims were struck out because social services enjoy statutory immunity
options.feedback: There is no statutory immunity. The claims failed because the court found no common law duty arose on the facts and on policy grounds.

oscoa_references: X (Minors) v Bedfordshire County Council [1995] 2 AC 633

question: Section 1 of the Law Reform (Contributory Negligence) Act 1945 provides that where a claimant suffers damage partly as a result of their own fault, damages may be:

options.text: Eliminated entirely if the claimant was more than 50% responsible
options.feedback: The Act allows reduction but not necessarily elimination. A 100% reduction is not permissible — St George v Home Office [2008].

options.text: Reduced to the extent the court thinks just and equitable having regard to the claimant’s share in the responsibility for the damage
options.feedback: Correct. Law Reform (Contributory Negligence) Act 1945, s.1 — damages are reduced to such extent as the court thinks just and equitable, based on the claimant’s share of responsibility for the damage.

options.text: Reduced by a fixed percentage determined by statutory guidelines
options.feedback: There is no statutory tariff. The assessment is discretionary and fact-sensitive.

options.text: Awarded in full regardless of the claimant’s fault, with a costs penalty
options.feedback: The Act specifically provides for reduction of damages, not costs penalties alone. The claimant’s contributory fault directly reduces recovery.

question: The claimant was not wearing a seatbelt when the defendant negligently crashed into her car. She suffered head injuries that she would not have sustained had she been belted. Applying Froom v Butcher [1976] QB 286, what reduction is likely?

options.text: No reduction — the defendant’s negligence was the sole cause of the accident
options.feedback: Contributory negligence looks at the claimant’s contribution to their own damage, not to the accident itself — Jones v Livox Quarries Ltd [1952].

options.text: 15–25% reduction — the failure to wear a seatbelt contributed to the severity of the injuries but not to the accident
options.feedback: Correct. Froom v Butcher [1976] QB 286 — where the failure to wear a seatbelt made a material contribution to the injuries, a reduction of 20–25% is typical. If wearing a belt would have prevented the injury entirely, 25%.

options.text: 50% reduction — the claimant is equally responsible for the injuries
options.feedback: The defendant’s negligence remained the primary cause. Seatbelt cases typically attract a 15–25% reduction, not 50%.

options.text: The claim is barred by volenti non fit injuria
options.feedback: Volenti requires voluntary acceptance of risk. Failure to wear a seatbelt is contributory negligence, not volenti.

oscoa_references: Jones v Livox Quarries Ltd [1952] 2 QB 608

question: A quarry worker rides on the tow bar of a vehicle in the quarry, contrary to instructions. Another vehicle, negligently driven by a colleague, crashes into him from behind. The worker suffers injury. Applying Jones v Livox Quarries Ltd [1952] 2 QB 608:

options.text: The claim fails — the worker’s breach of instruction is volenti
options.feedback: The case was decided on contributory negligence, not volenti.

options.text: The worker’s damages are reduced — his action contributed to the damage (the severity of the injury) even though it did not contribute to the accident
options.feedback: Correct. Jones v Livox Quarries Ltd [1952] 2 QB 608 — contributory negligence requires the claimant to have contributed to their own damage, not necessarily to the accident. Riding on the tow bar made the injuries worse.

options.text: The worker recovers in full — the colleague’s negligent driving was the sole cause of both the accident and the damage
options.feedback: While the colleague caused the accident, the worker’s own conduct contributed to the damage suffered. Damages were reduced.

options.text: The claim fails entirely — the worker’s fault was the predominant cause of the injury
options.feedback: The 1945 Act does not permit a 100% reduction. The worker recovered, albeit with a deduction for contributory negligence.

oscoa_references: Jones v Livox Quarries Ltd [1952] 2 QB 608

question: In Morris v Murray [1991] 2 QB 6, the plaintiff accepted a flight with a pilot he knew to be very drunk. The plane crashed and the plaintiff was injured. On the defence of volenti:

options.text: Volenti failed — the plaintiff did not expressly consent to the specific risk of a crash
options.feedback: Explicit consent to each specific risk is not required. The plaintiff voluntarily accepted the general risk of injury from the drunken flight.

options.text: Volenti succeeded — the plaintiff knew the pilot was drunk, freely agreed to fly, and implicitly accepted the risk of injury
options.feedback: Correct. Morris v Murray [1991] 2 QB 6 — volenti requires (1) knowledge of the risk, and (2) free and voluntary agreement to accept it. Both were satisfied: the plaintiff knew the pilot was drunk and willingly joined the flight.

options.text: Volenti failed — drunkenness vitiates consent in all circumstances
options.feedback: It was the pilot who was drunk, not the plaintiff. The plaintiff’s consent was not vitiated by alcohol.

options.text: The defence of contributory negligence applied instead, reducing the plaintiff’s damages
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal held volenti applied as a complete defence. This was one of the rare cases where volenti succeeded.

question: Section 149 of the Road Traffic Act 1988 prevents a defendant from relying on the defence of volenti non fit injuria against a passenger in a motor vehicle. — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: Correct. Road Traffic Act 1988 s.149 — the defence of volenti non fit injuria (voluntary acceptance of risk) cannot be used against a passenger in a motor vehicle. The defendant cannot say the passenger accepted the risk of negligent driving.

options.text: False
options.feedback: The statement is true. Section 149 RTA 1988 expressly removes the defence of volenti in motor vehicle cases for passengers.

question: Two experienced shot-firers at a quarry decide to ignore statutory safety regulations and test explosives without taking shelter. They are both injured in the explosion. One sues his employer. Applying ICI Ltd v Shatwell [1965] AC 656:

options.text: The employer is vicariously liable — the employees were acting in the course of employment
options.feedback: The House of Lords held that volenti applied. The employees’ deliberate breach of regulations was outside the scope of the employer’s duty.

options.text: The defence of volenti succeeds — both employees fully appreciated the risk and deliberately chose to ignore the safety regulations; the employer was not liable
options.feedback: Correct. ICI Ltd v Shatwell [1965] AC 656 — where experienced employees with full knowledge of the risk deliberately choose to flout safety regulations, volenti applies as a complete defence. The employer is not vicariously liable.

options.text: The employer is liable but the claimant’s damages are reduced for contributory negligence
options.feedback: The House of Lords went further — volenti applied as a complete defence, not merely contributory negligence. The employees had voluntarily accepted the risk.

options.text: The employer is strictly liable for all workplace injuries under the Factories Act
options.feedback: The employer’s liability was not strict. The employees’ conduct broke the chain and established volenti.

question: In Pitts v Hunt [1991] 1 QB 24, an 18-year-old pillion passenger on a motorbike encouraged the rider to drive dangerously while they were both drunk. The rider crashed and the passenger was seriously injured. The illegality defence:

options.text: Failed — a passenger can always recover from a negligent driver
options.feedback: The defence succeeded because of the claimant’s close participation in the illegality.

options.text: Succeeded — the claimant was a knowing participant in a joint illegal enterprise and his claim was barred
options.feedback: Correct. Pitts v Hunt [1991] 1 QB 24 — where the claimant was an active participant in the illegal enterprise (encouraging drunk and dangerous driving), the illegality defence (ex turpi causa) barred the claim entirely.

options.text: Resulted in a reduction of damages for contributory negligence, not a complete bar
options.feedback: The court applied the illegality defence as a complete bar, not merely contributory negligence.

options.text: Was not pleaded because volenti was the applicable defence
options.feedback: The court applied illegality (ex turpi causa). Volenti was an alternative but the illegal nature of the enterprise was the basis of the decision.

question: In Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42, the Supreme Court reformulated the illegality defence. The new tripartite test asks whether allowing the claim would be inconsistent with the purpose of the prohibition, would undermine public policy, and whether denial of the claim would be a proportionate response. Which of the following is NOT a consideration?

options.text: The underlying purpose of the prohibition which has been transgressed
options.feedback: This IS a consideration under the first limb of Patel v Mirza.

options.text: Any other relevant public policy on which the denial of the claim may have an impact
options.feedback: This IS a consideration under the second limb of the Patel test.

options.text: Whether the defendant has been convicted of a criminal offence in relation to the same facts
options.feedback: Correct — this is NOT a formal consideration under the Patel test. While criminal conduct is relevant, the test does not require a prior conviction.

options.text: Whether denial of the claim would be a proportionate response to the illegality
options.feedback: This IS a consideration — proportionality is the third limb of the Patel v Mirza test.

question: A woman with severe schizophrenia, in a psychotic episode, stabs her mother to death. She later sues the NHS trust for negligence in failing to detain her under the Mental Health Act 1983. The NHS trust argues the claim is barred by illegality because the claimant killed her mother. Applying Henderson v Dorset Healthcare University NHS Foundation Trust [2020] UKSC 43:

options.text: The claim is barred — a claimant cannot rely on their own criminal act (manslaughter)
options.feedback: The Supreme Court held the claim was not barred. The Patel v Mirza proportionality test was applied.

options.text: The claim is not barred — applying Patel v Mirza, barring the claim would be disproportionate; the claimant’s responsibility was diminished by severe mental illness, and the public would not be offended by allowing the claim
options.feedback: Correct. Henderson v Dorset Healthcare University NHS Foundation Trust [2020] UKSC 43 — applying the Patel v Mirza tripartite test, the Supreme Court held barring the claim would be disproportionate. The claimant’s personal responsibility was minimal due to schizophrenia.

options.text: The claim is barred — the Mental Health Act 1983 provides the sole remedy
options.feedback: The MHA 1983 does not exclude common law claims. The Supreme Court considered the claim on its merits.

options.text: The claim is barred — the negligence of the NHS trust was not causally connected to the killing
options.feedback: The Supreme Court decided the case on the illegality defence, not causation. The negligence was assumed for the purposes of the appeal.

oscoa_references: Henderson v Dorset Healthcare University NHS Foundation Trust [2020] UKSC 43

question: In Collins v Wilcock [1984] 1 WLR 1172, the court considered the elements of battery. Which statement correctly reflects the law?

options.text: Battery requires proof of hostile intent by the defendant
options.feedback: Collins v Wilcock specifically held that ‘hostile intent’ is not a required element of the tort of battery.

options.text: Battery is the direct, intentional, and unlawful touching of another person — hostility is not required
options.feedback: Correct. Collins v Wilcock [1984] 1 WLR 1172 — battery requires direct, intentional, unlawful touching. ‘Hostile intent’ is not an element; the touching must simply go beyond what is generally acceptable in ordinary life.

options.text: Any touching of another person, however slight, constitutes battery
options.feedback: Not all touching is unlawful. Ordinary social contact impliedly consented to (e.g. jostling in a crowd) does not constitute battery.

options.text: Battery requires proof of physical injury
options.feedback: Battery is actionable per se — no proof of damage or injury is required. The unlawful touching itself constitutes the tort.

question: In R v Ireland; R v Burstow [1998] AC 147, the House of Lords held that silent telephone calls could constitute an assault. This established that:

options.text: Physical gesture is always required for assault
options.feedback: The House of Lords held the opposite — words alone, or even silence, can create reasonable apprehension of immediate battery.

options.text: Words alone, including silent telephone calls, can constitute an assault if they cause the victim to apprehend immediate unlawful force
options.feedback: Correct. R v Ireland; R v Burstow [1998] AC 147 — the House of Lords held that words, and even silent telephone calls, can constitute an assault where they cause the victim to apprehend immediate unlawful personal violence.

options.text: Silent calls are assault only if the caller is physically nearby
options.feedback: The caller’s physical location was not determinative. The question was whether the victim reasonably apprehended immediate violence.

options.text: Assault requires proof of actual physical contact
options.feedback: Assault does not require physical contact — that is battery. Assault is about the apprehension of imminent unlawful contact.

oscoa_references: R v Ireland; R v Burstow [1998] AC 147
