options.text: Provisional damages allow the claimant to recover twice for the same injury
options.feedback: Provisional damages do not permit double recovery. They allow for later review if a specific contingency occurs.

options.text: Provisional damages allow the court to award damages on the assumption the deterioration will NOT occur, but permit the claimant to return for further damages if the epilepsy materialises — avoiding the unfairness of lump sum speculation
options.feedback: Correct. Provisional damages allow the claimant to receive compensation on the basis that the future deterioration will not occur, but preserve the right to return for further compensation if the specified condition develops. This avoids having to speculate on the percentage chance at trial and is a valuable tactic where future deterioration is possible but uncertain.

options.text: Provisional damages guarantee the claimant a minimum lump sum regardless of whether deterioration occurs
options.feedback: Provisional damages award the base level of compensation. If deterioration does not occur, the claimant does not receive further sums. The strategy is about preserving the right to return, not guaranteeing a minimum.

options.text: Provisional damages are available for all types of tort, including defamation
options.feedback: Provisional damages are primarily for personal injury cases where there is a chance of serious future deterioration. They are not available for all torts.

question: In Shelfer v City of London Electric Lighting Co [1895] 1 Ch 287, the Court of Appeal set out criteria for when damages may be awarded in lieu of an injunction to restrain a continuing nuisance. Which of the following is NOT a Shelfer criterion?

options.text: The injury to the claimant’s legal rights is small
options.feedback: This IS one of the Shelfer criteria — the injury to the claimant’s rights must be small.

options.text: The injury is capable of being estimated in money
options.feedback: This IS one of the criteria — the harm must be quantifiable in financial terms.

options.text: The injury can be adequately compensated by a small money payment
options.feedback: This IS one of the criteria — the compensation must be modest.

options.text: The defendant must lack the financial means to comply with an injunction
options.feedback: Correct — this is NOT a Shelfer criterion. The defendant’s financial position does not affect the Shelfer analysis. The criteria focus on the nature and scale of the injury to the claimant, not the defendant’s means.

oscoa_references: Shelfer v City of London Electric Lighting Co [1895] 1 Ch 287

question: A motor racing circuit has caused noise nuisance to a nearby resident for years. The resident seeks an injunction to stop the racing. The circuit provides significant employment and community benefit. Applying Lawrence v Fen Tigers [2014] UKSC 13:

options.text: An injunction must be granted — nuisance creates an automatic right to injunctive relief
options.feedback: There is no automatic right to an injunction. The Supreme Court confirmed the court has a broad discretion to award damages in lieu.

options.text: The court has a broad discretion to award damages in lieu of an injunction — the Shelfer criteria are not a rigid code and the public interest may justify damages instead of an injunction
options.feedback: Correct. Lawrence v Fen Tigers [2014] UKSC 13 — the Shelfer criteria are useful but not a statutory straitjacket. The court may award damages in lieu of an injunction where the public interest and the circumstances justify it, even if the Shelfer conditions are not strictly met.

options.text: An injunction is automatic unless the defendant can prove they have planning permission
options.feedback: Planning permission is relevant but not determinative in either direction. The court retains discretion.

options.text: The resident must accept damages because the Shelfer criteria are statutory
options.feedback: The Shelfer criteria are not statutory. Lawrence v Fen Tigers confirmed they are guidelines and should not be applied mechanically.

oscoa_references: Shelfer v City of London Electric Lighting Co [1895] 1 Ch 287

question: Section 33 of the Limitation Act 1980 gives the court a discretion to disapply the primary limitation period in personal injury cases. Under Horton v Sadler [2006] UKHL 27, which factor does the court NOT consider?

options.text: The length of and reasons for the delay
options.feedback: This IS a factor the court must consider under s.33.

options.text: The effect of the delay on the cogency of the evidence
options.feedback: This IS a factor — whether evidence has deteriorated or become unavailable is critical.

options.text: The conduct of the defendant after the cause of action arose
options.feedback: This IS a factor — the defendant’s conduct is relevant to the exercise of the discretion.

options.text: Whether the claimant has alternative sources of compensation, such as insurance
options.feedback: Correct — the availability of insurance or other compensation is NOT a listed factor under s.33, though it may be tangentially relevant. The statutory factors focus on the prejudice caused by delay and the reasons for it.

question: A victim of sexual assault brings a claim for battery 10 years after the assault. The defendant argues the claim is statute-barred. Applying A v Hoare [2008] UKHL 6:

options.text: The claim is automatically barred — the 6-year limitation for tort claims has expired
options.feedback: The House of Lords held that s.33 applies to intentional torts, not just negligence. The court can extend time.

options.text: The court may exercise its s.33 discretion to allow the claim to proceed — s.33 applies to intentional trespass to the person (including sexual assault), and courts are encouraged to take a more liberal approach in such cases
options.feedback: Correct. A v Hoare [2008] UKHL 6 — s.33 of the Limitation Act 1980 applies to intentional trespass to the person (assault, battery). The House of Lords overruled earlier authority to the contrary. In sexual abuse cases, courts should apply s.33 more liberally, recognising the reasons why victims may delay bringing claims.

options.text: The claim is barred — criminal acts have no limitation period but civil claims always do
options.feedback: The distinction between criminal and civil limitation periods is not absolute. Section 33 provides a mechanism to extend civil limitation in appropriate cases.

options.text: The claim can proceed only if the defendant was convicted of a criminal offence
options.feedback: There is no requirement for a prior criminal conviction. The s.33 discretion is exercised on the civil evidence before the court.

question: Section 14 of the Limitation Act 1980 defines the ‘date of knowledge’ for the purposes of postponing the limitation period. For a claim to be brought, the claimant must know that the injury was significant, attributable to the defendant’s act/omission, and the identity of the defendant. A claimant seeking to postpone limitation would argue:

options.text: The limitation period never runs against a claimant who is in continuing treatment
options.feedback: There is no such blanket rule. The date of knowledge is a factual assessment, not automatic.

options.text: The claimant did not have knowledge of the ‘attribution’ — they did not know and could not reasonably have been expected to know that their injury was attributable to the defendant’s negligence
options.feedback: Correct. LA 1980 s.14(1)(b) — the claimant must have knowledge that the injury was attributable in whole or in part to the act or omission alleged to constitute negligence. If the connection between injury and defendant’s conduct was not reasonably discoverable, the limitation period starts later.

options.text: The claimant was abroad during the limitation period
options.feedback: Absence from the jurisdiction is dealt with under different provisions (LA 1980 s.28). It is not the primary mechanism for postponing limitation.

options.text: The limitation period is 10 years for all latent damage cases
options.feedback: There is a 10-year long-stop for product liability under CPA 1987 s.11A, but not for all personal injury claims. The standard limitation period is 3 years from the date of knowledge or date of accrual.

question: A defendant in a defamation claim wishes to challenge the ‘serious harm’ threshold at the earliest possible stage to minimise costs. What is the most effective procedural tactic?

options.text: File a full defence pleading truth and honest opinion simultaneously
options.feedback: Filing a full defence commits the defendant to costly preparation. A preliminary issue approach is more cost-effective.

options.text: Apply for trial of a preliminary issue on serious harm — if the court finds no serious harm, the claim is struck out without the need for a full trial
options.feedback: Correct. Following Lachaux v Independent Print Ltd [2019] UKSC 27, the serious harm requirement under DA 2013 s.1 is a meaningful threshold. A defendant may apply under CPR 3.1 for determination of serious harm as a preliminary issue. If successful, this avoids the costs of a full trial on liability and defences. This is a powerful tactical loophole.

options.text: Make a Part 36 offer to settle immediately
options.feedback: A Part 36 offer is a costs protection mechanism, but does not address the threshold issue directly. A preliminary issue application is more targeted.

options.text: Ignore the claim until the limitation period expires
options.feedback: Ignoring a claim risks default judgment. Proactive case management is essential.

oscoa_references: Lachaux v Independent Print Ltd [2019] UKSC 27

question: A newspaper publishes an article that mistakenly accuses a local councillor of taking bribes. The newspaper realises the error within 24 hours. What is the most advantageous tactical response under the Defamation Act 1996?

options.text: Publish a unilateral correction and hope the councillor does not sue
options.feedback: A unilateral correction provides no legal protection. The offer of amends procedure provides statutory benefits.

options.text: Make an offer of amends under DA 1996 ss.2–4 — offer to publish a suitable correction and apology and pay compensation; if accepted, it is a complete defence and caps damages and costs
options.feedback: Correct. Defamation Act 1996 ss.2–4 — an offer of amends is a tactical loophole that can significantly limit the defendant’s exposure. If made promptly and properly, it: (a) operates as a complete defence if accepted and performed, (b) caps the level of damages, and (c) limits costs exposure.

options.text: Deny liability and force the claimant to prove serious harm at trial
options.feedback: While serious harm may be challenged, this approach exposes the newspaper to higher costs and damages than a well-timed offer of amends.

options.text: Claim the defence of qualified privilege under the DA 1996
options.feedback: Qualified privilege requires a duty to communicate and a corresponding interest in receiving the information. A factual error about bribery would unlikely attract privilege protection.

question: In Sturges v Bridgman (1879) 11 Ch D 852, a doctor built a consulting room next to a confectioner’s kitchen that had operated for many years. The noise from the kitchen’s pestles and mortars disturbed his consultations. The confectioner argued the doctor ‘came to the nuisance.’ The Court of Appeal held:

options.text: The defence succeeded — the doctor knew about the confectioner’s business and chose to build next door
options.feedback: The Court rejected this argument. Coming to the nuisance is not a defence.

options.text: Coming to the nuisance is NOT a defence — the doctor could still sue; the fact that the confectioner was there first did not give it immunity from nuisance liability
options.feedback: Correct. Sturges v Bridgman (1879) 11 Ch D 852 — coming to the nuisance is not a defence. The character of the neighbourhood at the time of the nuisance (not at the time the claimant arrived) is the relevant factor. This is a fundamental loophole/rule that prevents nuisance from being a ‘first in time’ right.

options.text: The confectioner won because the doctor had consented to the noise by buying the property
options.feedback: The court allowed the doctor’s claim. Consent was not implied merely from buying property in the vicinity.

options.text: The confectioner was liable because its operations had only been running for five years — the rule applies only to uses under 10 years old
options.feedback: There is no such temporal limit. The principle that ‘coming to the nuisance’ is not a defence applies regardless of how long the defendant’s use has operated.

question: A factory has emitted a particular type of noise and vibration for 30 years without complaint. A new neighbour moves in and sues in nuisance. The factory argues it has acquired a prescriptive right to emit the noise by 20+ years of uninterrupted use. What is the legal position?

options.text: The defence of prescription is automatic after 20 years — the factory is immune from all nuisance claims
options.feedback: Prescription is not automatic, even after 20 years. The claimant must show that the use was exercised as of right (nec vi, nec clam, nec precario — without force, secrecy, or permission).

options.text: Prescription (20+ years’ uninterrupted use exercised openly and as of right) may provide a defence to nuisance — but the factory must prove the use was sufficient to give the previous owner a potential claim that was not pursued
options.feedback: Correct. A prescriptive right may be acquired after 20 years of uninterrupted, open use exercised as of right (without force, secrecy, or permission). The factory must show the previous landowner could have sued during that period but did not. This is a niche but potentially effective loophole defence.

options.text: Prescription only applies to rights of way, not to nuisance claims
options.feedback: Prescription can apply to some forms of nuisance, though it is subject to strict requirements and is not easily established.

options.text: The defence always fails because nuisance is a continuing tort with no limitation period
options.feedback: While each fresh occurrence of nuisance gives a new cause of action, prescriptive rights may arise where the interference has continued for 20 years without challenge.

question: A company wishes to structure its workforce to minimise the risk of vicarious liability for torts committed by workers. Following Barclays Bank plc v Various Claimants [2020] UKSC 13, which factor is most important?

options.text: Drafting a written contract that labels the worker as an independent contractor
options.feedback: Following Autoclenz Ltd v Belcher [2011] UKSC 41, the label in the contract is not determinative. Courts look at the substance of the relationship.

options.text: Ensuring genuine operational autonomy — the worker determines their own methods, bears financial risk, provides their own equipment, and works for multiple clients
options.feedback: Correct. The substance of the relationship is key — per Barclays Bank [2020] and Autoclenz Ltd v Belcher [2011]. Genuine operational autonomy, bearing financial risk, providing own equipment, and working for multiple clients are strong indicators of independent contractor status rather than employment.

options.text: Requiring the worker to carry their own insurance
options.feedback: Insurance is a factor but not determinative. The overall substance of the relationship is what courts assess.

options.text: Paying the worker through a limited company rather than PAYE
options.feedback: Payment structure is one factor among many. It does not, by itself, determine the employment status. See Uber BV v Aslam [2021] UKSC 5.

oscoa_references: Barclays Bank plc v Various Claimants [2020] UKSC 13

question: A company engages independent contractors and takes indemnities from them to cover any liability arising from their torts. What is the primary legal limitation on relying on such indemnities?

options.text: Indemnities from independent contractors are void as against public policy
options.feedback: Indemnities are not automatically void. They are subject to reasonableness under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977.

options.text: The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 — indemnities may be subject to the requirement of reasonableness, particularly where the contractor is dealing on the company’s written standard terms
options.feedback: Correct. The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 applies to indemnities given by persons acting in the course of business. An indemnity in the company’s standard terms must satisfy the reasonableness requirement. A court may declare an unreasonable indemnity unenforceable.

options.text: Indemnities are only enforceable if the contractor is a limited company
options.feedback: The enforceability of indemnities does not depend on the contractor’s corporate form. It depends on the UCTA 1977 reasonableness test.

options.text: Indemnities are automatically void under the Employers’ Liability (Compulsory Insurance) Act 1969
options.feedback: The 1969 Act applies to employees, not independent contractors. It prohibits insurance-based indemnities against employees but does not directly affect independent contractor arrangements.

question: A claimant files a claim in negligence against a local authority for failing to protect her from a third-party attack, based on facts indistinguishable from Michael v Chief Constable of South Wales Police [2015] UKSC 2. The defendant’s most effective procedural response is:

options.text: File a defence and proceed to a full trial on the facts
options.feedback: Where the claim has no legal basis on binding authority, a full trial is unnecessary and wasteful.

options.text: Apply to strike out the claim under CPR 3.4 (no reasonable grounds) or for summary judgment under CPR 24.2 (no real prospect of success) — the claim is bound by existing Supreme Court authority
options.feedback: Correct. Where the claim is bound to fail on the basis of existing binding authority, an early strike-out or summary judgment application is the most effective tactical response. Robinson v CC West Yorkshire [2018] UKSC 4 supports the use of strike-out where the duty of care question can be resolved on authority alone.

options.text: Apply for an interim injunction to stop the claim proceeding
options.feedback: An interim injunction is not the appropriate procedural mechanism for dismissing a claim. Strike-out or summary judgment is the correct route.

options.text: Agree to a stay of proceedings pending further Supreme Court guidance
options.feedback: Where existing Supreme Court authority is directly on point, a stay is unnecessary. The claim can be disposed of summarily.

oscoa_references: Michael v Chief Constable of South Wales Police [2015] UKSC 2
Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018] UKSC 4

question: The test for summary judgment under CPR 24.2 is that the claimant has ‘no real prospect of succeeding on the claim or issue.’ Which of the following correctly describes this threshold?

options.text: The claimant must prove their case on a balance of probabilities at the summary judgment stage
options.feedback: The summary judgment threshold is lower — the defendant must show the claimant has no real (as opposed to fanciful) prospect of success.

options.text: The test is whether the claim has a ‘realistic’ as opposed to ‘fanciful’ prospect of success — the court does not conduct a mini-trial but may assess the evidence
options.feedback: Correct. Under CPR 24.2, summary judgment may be granted where the claimant has no real prospect of success — meaning the prospect must be realistic, not fanciful. The court avoids conducting a mini-trial but may evaluate the available evidence to determine if there is a triable issue.

options.text: Summary judgment can only be granted if the claim is an abuse of process
options.feedback: Abuse of process is a separate ground under CPR 3.4. Summary judgment under CPR 24.2 has a different threshold based on the prospects of success.

options.text: The defendant must prove the claim is fraudulent to obtain summary judgment
options.feedback: Fraud is not required. The defendant need only show there is no real prospect of the claim succeeding on its merits.

oscoa_references: JSC BTA Bank v Ablyazov [2013] EWCA Civ 928

question: The principle in Henderson v Henderson (1843) 3 Hare 100 provides that parties must bring their whole case before the court in a single set of proceedings. What is the practical consequence?

options.text: A party can bring separate claims arising from the same facts in sequential proceedings as long as different causes of action are pleaded
options.feedback: This is precisely what Henderson v Henderson prohibits. Parties must bring all claims, not split their case.

options.text: A party who could and should have raised a claim or issue in earlier proceedings but failed to do so may be barred from raising it in later proceedings — it is an abuse of process
options.feedback: Correct. Henderson v Henderson (1843) 3 Hare 100 — parties are expected to bring their entire case at once. Claims or issues that could and should have been raised in earlier proceedings but were not may be struck out as an abuse of process in subsequent proceedings.

options.text: The principle only applies to claims involving fraud
options.feedback: Henderson v Henderson applies generally across all civil proceedings, not only to fraud claims.

options.text: The principle was abolished by the Civil Procedure Rules 1998
options.feedback: The principle survives under the CPR. It is specifically recognised as a ground for strike-out under CPR 3.4 as an abuse of process.

question: A tenant and a landlord litigate a rent review dispute. The court determines the proper construction of the rent review clause. Years later, the landlord brings a new claim for a different rent review period, but the key issue depends on the same clause construction. Applying Arnold v National Westminster Bank plc [1991] 2 AC 93:

options.text: The new claim is permitted — different review periods raise different issues
options.feedback: Where the identical issue of construction has already been determined, issue estoppel may bar its re-litigation.

options.text: The landlord is estopped from re-litigating the construction of the clause — issue estoppel prevents the same issue (though between the same parties in different proceedings) from being re-litigated
options.feedback: Correct. Arnold v National Westminster Bank plc [1991] 2 AC 93 — issue estoppel prevents the re-litigation of an identical issue that has already been finally determined between the same parties. The landlord cannot re-argue the construction of the clause, even in a claim for a different period.

options.text: The new claim is permitted because the clause may be interpreted differently over time
options.feedback: The construction of a contractual clause is a question of law. Once determined, it binds the parties in subsequent litigation unless there are special circumstances.

options.text: The new claim is barred by the Limitation Act 1980, not issue estoppel
options.feedback: While limitation may also be relevant, the primary procedural bar is issue estoppel (and possibly Henderson v Henderson abuse of process).

oscoa_references: Arnold v National Westminster Bank plc [1991] 2 AC 93
